items.passage: Evolution is the slow process by which living things change over many generations. New traits often appear by chance, and animals or plants with helpful traits tend to survive and have more young. Over millions of years, small changes can add up to large differences. Some species look very different from their ancient relatives, while others stay almost the same.

Soil erosion happens when wind or water carries away the top layer of soil from the land. Farms can lose this rich layer very quickly when fields are left bare between crops without cover. Trees and grasses help hold the soil in place with their roots. Without these plants, heavy rain can easily wash large amounts of good soil away into nearby rivers.

Perception is the way the brain takes in information from the senses and turns it into useful meaning. When a person looks at an object, the eyes send signals to the brain, which then decides what is being seen. Sometimes the brain fills in missing details based on past experience. This is why two people can look at the same picture and see slightly different things.

In medieval Europe, guilds were groups of skilled workers who came together to protect their trade. Members of a guild learned the same craft, set fair prices for their goods, and trained young apprentices. Guilds also helped sick or poor members and supported their families during hard times. Many of the rules from these old groups still shape how some modern professions are organized.

Meteor impacts happen when a large rock from space crashes into a planet at very high speed. These collisions can create huge round craters and throw dust into the air for many months. On Earth, most meteors burn up in the atmosphere before reaching the ground. However, a few large impacts in the distant past changed the climate and even shaped the path of life.

The solar system contains the Sun, eight planets, and many smaller bodies like moons, comets, and asteroids. The Sun's strong gravity holds all of these objects in orbit around it. Planets closer to the Sun, such as Mercury and Venus, are made mostly of rock. The four outer planets are much larger and are made mostly of gas or thick ice.
